data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "The collapse resulted from a cascading failure of bronze-alloy supply chains when Cypriot copper-tin trade routes were disrupted by multi-vector piracy and political fragmentation, rendering chariot-based warfare and elite gift-exchange economies unsustainable","mechanism": "Bronze metallurgy required precise copper-tin ratios from geographically separated sources; disruption of Cypriot-smelting-to-Levantine-port logistics created metal scarcity that invalidated the military and ideological foundations of palace economies, while Egypt's Nubian gold and local copper reserves permitted bronze substitution and retrenchment","scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt proper", "Mesopotamian interior", "Central Mediterranean"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Archaeological evidence of continued large-scale bronze production in palaces after 1200 BC", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Discovery of alternative tin sources (e.g., Afghani, Iberian) sustaining regional metallurgy post-collapse", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Egyptian texts describing bronze rather than gold/silver as the critical shortage material", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Emphasizes materialist infrastructure over climate or invasion; treats Egypt's survival as resource-geographic luck rather than institutional superiority. Vulnerable to反驳 if tin accessibility turns out to have been less constrained than LBA trade patterns suggest."}
